reasoning: With only about two months remaining in 2025, the base rate for any CEO to unexpectedly depart in such a short window is exceedingly low (typically <1%). For Elon Musk, despite the pressure from investors over his split focus and involvement in the DOGE commission, recent evidence points very strongly against an imminent exit. In September 2025, Tesla proposed a new 10-year compensation package tailored to keep him at the company, specifically requiring him to remain CEO or a senior executive for at least two years to collect initial tranches. Moreover, both Musk and the Tesla board have unambiguously denied rumors of a CEO search. A sudden transition before December 31, 2025, would require an unexpectedly hasty process or an abrupt disqualifying event. Thus, a low probability of 3% is highly appropriate, aligning with the late-October market estimate.
